data.title: Aeva Technologies: A Promising Vision Facing Harsh Realities
data.description:
Aeva Technologies (NASDAQ:AEVA) continues to seek a place among the most advanced LiDAR technology developers, but despite the visions of autonomous mobility and next-generation sensory platforms, investors still have many unanswered questions. The company’s Aeva Day event and news about cooperation with LG Innotek were certainly a step in the right direction, but I think their practical value to investors remains vague. At the same time, financial health reflects the same old reality: high losses, high operating costs, low revenue, and still far from profitability.

Financial Health and Q2 2025

AEVA Technologies reported a record quarter. The company earned $5.5 million in revenue. At first glance, it may seem like a strong step forward, and don’t get me wrong, it is a good step. However, the company’s financial position still isn’t safe. Although operating losses decreased slightly ($34.9 million), AEVA is still burning cash and remains far from profitability.

It is even more surprising that the overall loss totaled $192.7 million. Why so much? Not because of direct business losses. This is more related to accounting entries, for example, the revaluation of existing warrant liabilities and the share‑subscription liability tied to the LG Innotek investment. In other words, these are mostly paper losses, not direct cash losses. However, they show that the company’s reported profit or loss is highly sensitive to share price movements and other non-operational variables.

Another important factor is the cash situation. At the end of the quarter, AEVA held $49.8 million in cash and short-term investments. Does that sound like a lot? Not really. The company burned through approximately $60.6 million in the first half of the year, about $10 million per month. At this pace, cash could run out in 4-5 months. Yes, AEVA has a signed agreement to raise up to $125 million from investors, but this would require issuing new shares, which means existing shareholders could be diluted.

Another unpleasant fact is that AEVA's balance sheet shows negative equity: -$118 million. This means the company’s liabilities exceed its assets. Although mostly due to accounting adjustments, it’s still a serious warning signal that the financial position is weak. Negative equity is often seen in companies that struggle to stay viable over the long term.

Finally, although AEVA speaks about future technologies, advanced 4D LiDAR, and potential customers in the automotive sector, current revenue is mostly coming from small prototype orders, not large, recurring contracts. Without a stable cash flow or a visible path to profitability, this company still appears to be carrying a high-risk profile. Investors should be aware that AEVA may need to raise capital again, which could mean more shares, more dilution, and more uncertainty. But that’s my speculation.

Catalysts

AEVA is positioning itself as one of the technological leaders on the LiDAR market, but so far, I believe the development of the company is driven more by expectations than by evidence. However, there are currently some potential catalysts to distinguish, which, if realized, might help AEVA recover on the market at least for a short time or attract the attention of new investors.

The first of these is cooperation with the US defense industry. The company emphasizes that its technology meets the requirements for the Pentagon and that the company is actively involved in US defense sector decisions on advanced sensor systems. Defense is often distinguished by larger margins, longer contracts, and lower price pressure, so I believe any more serious order or partnership could significantly improve the results in the coming quarters. However, so far, it remains only a strategic direction without specific signed contracts or revenue from that segment.

Another possible catalyst is cooperation with the ZF Group, one of the world's largest car component manufacturers. AEVA hopes that its 4D LiDAR technology will become the cornerstone of other generations of autonomous driving systems. The company more recently announced a partnership with Daimler Truck, aiming to scale its 4D LiDAR into autonomous freight platforms. This sounds promising, but there is still no transparency on whether this partnership will result in actual mass production or signed orders. From what is known, it’s mostly a strategic agreement for now, which may or may not lead to commercial results anytime soon.

AEVA also highlights the technological flexibility of its LiDAR platforms. The company not only develops a car market, but also aims to adapt to industrial robots, unmanned aircraft, and smart cities. Theoretically, this increases the potential of the market, but in reality, these areas are further fragmented, with many competing technologies and different integration requirements. In addition, most of these segments are still developing regulatory systems, which means a long way to standardization and large-scale implementation.

From a short-term point of view, one of the main catalysts can become additional funding. If the company is able to successfully draw from its $125 million share purchase agreement without significant pressure on shares, it could stabilize liquidity and provide the ability to continue the development of operations without urgent cost reductions. However, it depends on market conditions and the appetite of investors, which is currently weak.

In conclusion, although AEVA has several potential growth catalysts, none of them have been clearly materialized. The value of the company on the market is still more based on technological promise than confirmed business performance, in my view.

Risks

There are inevitably many essential risks that affect both the short-term and long-term perspectives of the company. First of all, financial survival. AEVA is currently burning more than $10 million per month. At the end of Q2, the company had $49.8 million in cash and short-term investments. This could mean that without new capital raised, the company would last less than half a year. This circumstance might force the company to depend on additional funding, which directly poses a risk to current shareholders as the probability of dilution increases, as debt is not likely to be used in this case.

The company has a share sales agreement to raise up to $125 million. At first glance, it may seem like a financial safety net, but these funds would be raised through public share issues, and if it happens at the current (or lower) price, dilution of shareholders would be significant. This risk is particularly relevant to retail investors.

Another significant risk is the uncertainty around the market and the technology. AEVA is developing an advanced 4D LiDAR solution, but the autonomous driving sector still remains in the development phase. Despite high expectations over the past decade, most automakers have postponed autonomous systems implementation plans due to technical and regulatory challenges. For example, Tesla has gone away from LiDAR systems by choosing other methods (like cameras and AI combinations). This shows that LiDAR is not the only way to autonomous driving, and AEVA is currently building its business around this one direction, which might not be sustainable in my opinion.

Technological competition makes the situation even more difficult. Players like Luminar, Innoviz, or Hesai offer their solutions and have already established partnership models with car manufacturers. Although declaring cooperation with the ZF Group, AEVA has no known serial integration into mass production yet. In addition, the company does not have notable long-term contracts, which could mean that even after a record quarter, such as Q2, financial performance is unlikely to be sustainable. Small prototype orders cannot ensure business stability, in my view.

Finally, there is a risk of management. AEVA’s leadership continues to focus on the "vision", but specific financial results do not justify trust in the quick transition to a sustainable growth history. The company's strategy is still focused on a distant future, where LiDAR will become a market standard, but there is no guarantee at the moment that this will actually happen.

Valuation

Aeva Technologies is currently reaching the overvalued zone, especially if we compare it to other companies operating in the same sector. The main distinctive indicator is the Price/Sales (TTM), which is 101.35. This means that investors pay more than $100 for each $1 of revenue when the sector average varies by about 5 to 10. For example, EVLV has 9.26, NSSC 6.25, and OUST 9.33. This difference shows the strongly inflated expectations of the market.

The EV/Sales (TTM) ratio is also unfavorable—95.96. Compared to NSSC (5.63), LASR (4.55), or DAKT (0.92), AEVA looks extremely expensive even among other technology companies in the growth stage. Market valuation in this case does not reflect the existing volume or profitability of the business—a more speculative bet on commercial success in the distant future.

Price/Book reaches 15.52, which indicates that the market price is more than 15 times higher than the accounting value of the company. Other participants in the sector look much more moderate here: EVLV - 8.96, NSSC - 6.83, LASR - 4.80. I believe this does not have a clear fundamental basis and only reinforces the impression that the current price is not based on real financial results.

The AEVA looks significantly overvalued when looking at these main ratios. Such a situation can only survive with very strong technological breakthroughs or long-term investor faith.

Summary

AEVA Technologies currently remains one of the riskiest stories in the entire LiDAR sector, in my view. Despite the technological potential and several partnerships published, the company has no clear path to profitability, and the cash burn remains high. Even after a record Q2 2025, the company doesn't seem to be in a stable or strong position from a financial health perspective. Yes, the share price rose quite sharply if we look at YTD, but I simply do not see fundamental backing here.

The valuation remains high—around 100 times based on Price/Sales and EV/Sales. I think such numbers require a technological breakthrough in either rapid and exponential growth or great faith. None of these scenarios has yet been confirmed. In addition, the company has the opportunity to issue new shares in the near future for more than $100 million, which would increase the pressure on existing shareholders.

Despite several interesting technological elements - such as AEVA ATLAS or LG cooperation, the company's financial situation, slow commercial progress, and huge market optimism make this position extremely sensitive to correction. There are currently no clear catalysts that could justify the current valuation, in my opinion.

For my initial Seeking Alpha analysis of AEVA, I am rating the stock a SELL.

data.title: Tronox Holdings plc 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tronox Holdings plc in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Markel Group: Focus On Specialty Is Positive For The Long Term
data.description:
Markel Group Inc. (NYSE:MKL) just reported its second-quarter results on July 30th, and despite the 170% YoY increase in GAAP operating income, the results are not that good once broken down.

The whole increase in operating income has been caused by the appreciation of its publicly traded equities portfolio, albeit underperforming the +10% delivered by the S&P 500 during the quarter.

In the insurance segment, earned premiums grew by a modest 3%, and underwriting profit decreased because of unfavorable changes in loss reserves and higher expenses due to the recent reorganization.

On the positive side, Markel Ventures delivered 17% YoY operating income growth, and as anticipated in my previous coverage, the Series A Preferred shares have been redeemed.

As the company exits its less profitable insurance lines and the expense ratio decreases in the next quarters, I expect profitability to improve.

Without further ado, let's take a closer look at Markel's second-quarter results.

The Insurance Segment Needs to Improve

In the insurance segment, the core of the Markel Group, the company delivered yet another disappointing quarter, with operating income decreasing by 27.5% YoY.

However, there is a huge difference between the performance of the specialty insurance business, the fronting business, and the insurance-linked securities business.

In the specialty insurance business, where Markel insures a wide range of risks such as marine cargo, classic cars, and cyberattacks, among many others, operating income decreased by 51% YoY, and earned premiums have been flat for over two years, which is something that concerns me.

Despite the lower-than-expected impact from the January 2025 California wildfires and the good performance of the international business (+11% YoY earned premiums), the combined ratio increased to 96.9% (up from 93.8% a year ago) due to higher expenses related to the recent reorganization and the awful performance of the Global Reinsurance division and the executive insurance business.

The good news is that Markel stopped writing new policies in both divisions, and the reorganization costs are a one-time expense, so the combined ratio has room to improve.

After years of losing money with the Global Reinsurance business, its sale to Nationwide has just been announced, and considering that it was the division with the worst combined ratio (125.7% in Q2 2025), it will have a positive impact on the overall results going forward.

With the increase in court cases going after large-cap executives, Markel also decided to exit the directors and officers (D&O) liability insurance, which was priced too low:

Unfortunately, that business has now been written, and we're seeing the claims come through, and we actually saw an acceleration of those claims, probably during the course of 2023 and certainly during 2024, on those particular years of account.

Source: Simon Wilson (CEO Markel Insurance) - Q2 2025 EC.

In my view, this might be the right decision, since I have never seen this amount of lawsuits impact executives. I recently covered the Atkore Inc. (NYSE:ATKR) lawsuit, but there are many more examples. Just a few days ago, Fiserv, Inc. (NYSE:FI) joined the list.

As shown in the table below, Markel has been delivering a higher combined ratio than its peers for some time, and considering the lack of growth in earned premiums, the company has lost market share.

Following the poor performance of the specialty insurance segment, Markel recently started a corporate reorganization to simplify the structure and hold managers accountable at all levels.

We are going to call out P&Ls across the business, whether it be 60 units or probably slightly less than that. But in those P&Ls, we're going to have a business leader, a clear set of accounts where you understand what it is that you're responsible for.

And then, we're going to hold people to account to make sure that those P&Ls make sense.

Source: Simon Wilson (CEO Markel Insurance) - 2025 Annual Meeting.

In contrast with the multiple issues around Markel Insurance, operating income generated from State National and Nephilia grew by 25% YoY in Q2 to $56M.

The State National business, which generates fees in exchange for fronting and writing collateral protection, delivered small revenue growth but a substantial improvement in profitability (79.2% combined ratio), driving a 19% YoY operating income growth.

Given that State National started expanding its fronting services internationally last year, I expect the good performance to continue.

Nephilia, the investment fund division focused on climate risk, delivered an operating income of $6.7M (+92% YoY) due to higher management fees, and in my view, this is one of the most valuable parts of Markel's insurance business.

On top of the higher management fees, Markel is benefiting from the secular growth of the Cat bond market, with record issuances YTD.

Markel Ventures: The Positive News

One of the positive aspects of Markel's diversification is that when the insurance segment doesn't perform as expected, other parts of the business can compensate.

In Q2, Markel Ventures delivered 7% YoY revenue growth, of which almost half is because of the acquisitions of EPI and Valor, but the best news is the 17% YoY growth in operating income ($207.7M).

Organic growth (~4%) has just been slightly above inflation, but it is important to remember that Markel is exposed to cyclicality through its portfolio of privately owned companies.

While it is great news that activity and margins in construction services are improving, the transport business is going through a slowdown.

But this is not exclusive to Markel's companies (Weldship and Havco), since Old Dominion (NASDAQ:ODFL) just reported a 15.1% drop in operating income during Q2, and Saia (NASDAQ:SAIA) a 27.8% decrease.

All things considered, I believe the 17% YoY growth in operating income amid the uncertainty around tariffs has been the most positive surprise from Markel's Q2 results.

Equity Portfolio Appreciation, Flat Interest Income

On the investments part of Markel's business, the equity portfolio benefited from the positive market returns during the quarter, but interest income remained flat.

As older debt matures, the yield generated on long-term debt investments increases, but this is offset by lower rates in short-term maturities, which represent 20% of the fixed income portfolio.

Despite the $596M equity portfolio appreciation, Markel has significantly underperformed the S&P 500's 10% return during the quarter due to its lower exposure to the tech sector.

Nonetheless, I believe Markel's performance on the equity portfolio should be judged over longer periods, and the management has repeatedly stated that its goal is to generate reasonable returns with low risk. During periods of increased volatility, I expect Markel's portfolio to deliver smoother results due to its low beta.

Capital Allocation and Buybacks

On the capital allocation front, as expected, Markel repurchased its Series A preferred shares for $600M and spent another $99M in buybacks.

The redemption of the Series A preferred shares will reduce dividend expenses by $36M per year, and the share count decreased by 0.8% during Q2.

Given that Markel already spent $860M in buybacks during the first half of 2025, which is more than in any previous full year, there has been little room to increase the fixed income and equity portfolios.

Markel Ventures, after the two deals closed in June and September 2024, has not announced any further acquisitions.

Fairly Valued Stock

During the latest annual meeting, Markel presented its intrinsic value calculation, assigning a fair value per share of $2,610, which would represent a 30% upside from current prices.

Although the multiple plus sum of the parts approach seems appropriate to me, I have a bit of an issue with the 12x operating income multiple.

As I stated in my last coverage of Markel, its peers are trading at 12x net income, and the model I personally use, which is a pure multiple approach (assuming an average profitability for the equity portfolio instead of adding its market value), also accounts for a lower multiple.

In the model presented above, I am updating the estimates that I shared a year ago, and considering the run-off of the Global Reinsurance division (~10% of the premium volume), I expect lower growth in insurance profits during the next two years.

Although Markel will keep receiving premiums for up to three years due to multiyear contracts, the decrease will offset growth in other divisions.

However, the management is taking the right steps to improve profitability, and I have a positive view on the focus towards the specialty business, where Markel has its competitive advantage.

Where I do expect the positive performance to continue in the short term is in the international division (previously led by Simon Wilson), since Markel just closed the acquisition of MECO, a specialist marine insurer.

As the overall combined ratio improves due to lower expenses and growth rates accelerating in 2027, the valuation multiple could expand. For now, Markel is fairly priced.

Bottom Line

Markel Group delivered a positive surprise with the performance of its Ventures engine, but insurance, which is the core of the company, disappointed again.

I believe the management is taking the right steps to reverse the situation, but it will take time to see the results. The focus on specialty insurance, the simplification of the structure, and the increasing accountability will improve profitability.

Considering the sale of the Global Reinsurance division and the run-off of the D&O liability insurance, earned premiums will grow slowly in the next quarters, but this will have a positive impact on the combined ratio.

The stock is fairly priced, and it is true that the valuation multiple could expand over time, but Markel needs to deliver in the insurance segment.

data.title: East Group: Growth From Superior Location And Segment
data.description:
East Group Properties (NYSE:EGP) is an industrial REIT focused on sub-150K square foot warehouses in the Sunbelt.

The Buy Thesis

EGP has the best-positioned industrial assets in terms of both location and property specs. It's relatively limited competing supply sets EGP up for roughly 10% AFFO per share growth annually. The growth is not reflected in its 21.8X AFFO multiple, which makes it among the cheapest industrial REITs on a leverage-neutral basis. As this is my first focus article on EGP, it will be fairly comprehensive, covering the following topics:

  • EGP track record
  • Massive drop in AFFO multiple that makes it cheap today
  • Asset size/specs analysis
  • Asset submarket analysis
  • NAV valuation
  • AFFO multiple valuation relative to peers
  • Balance sheet
  • Acquisition/development growth pipeline
  • Organic growth
  • Risks
  • Overall outlook for the company/stock

East Group’s Track Record

Over the years, I have attended hundreds of meetings with management teams. REIT executives tend to have a certain blend of charisma and salesmanship. They generally tell the truth, but always with a bit of spin, placing their company in a positive light.

EGP is culturally different. They feel more like utility executives—practitioners with a focus on the very long term. Marshall Loeb (CEO) has run the company in an extremely conservative fashion, and some would even argue that leverage is too low, leaving some money on the table. However, this conservative approach has led to an extraordinary streak of consistent net operating income growth.

This 34-year window has had some rough times for the industrial sector, which had a downturn in the 2000 recession and got clobbered in the Financial Crisis.

EGP managed through these challenges with only the slightest downticks in NOI. At the same time, the company has been able to fully participate in boom periods, locking in NOI growth for the long term.

This impressive track record has caused EGP to consistently trade at premium multiples. I have wanted to buy EGP for many years, but didn’t until now because the stock traded well outside of our valuation parameters. In recent years, however, EGP’s stock went from a premium valuation to substantially undervalued.

AFFO Multiple From 34.8X to 21.8X

At the end of 2021, EGP traded at 36X trailing AFFO and 34.8X forward.

It was the peak of the industrial market as the REITs rode the highs from the COVID-driven logistics boom.

In hindsight, the market got a little too excited. It extrapolated growth from a one-time driver. Since that time, it has been all downhill. EGP’s stock price dropped from the $210.72 pictured above to today’s $163, where we just bought shares in the 2nd Market Capital High Yield Portfolio (2CHYP).

Over that same time period in which the market price got clobbered, FFO grew materially.

Specifically, FFO grew 31% from $1.62 in Q4'21 to $2.12 in Q1'25.

The simultaneous earnings growth and price declines took EGP from overvalued to significantly undervalued. We see this as a long-awaited opportunity to buy into a great company that is finally trading at a great price.

Industrial Sector Overview: Strengths and Concerns

Like most real estate sectors, the industrial sector experienced a surge in development activity brought about by the zero-interest rate environment following the pandemic. We discuss this dynamic more thoroughly here.

The pace of development is slowing considerably, but the sheer volume of completions in the last few years has taken vacancy up from record lows circa 2022 to just over 7% today.

The supply was well absorbed due to equally robust demand growth brought about by both industrial production and freight volumes.

Demand comes from a wide range of tenants.

Thus, even as vacancy ticked up, rental rates remained strong, with average rent per foot now over $10. 2026 looks stronger than 2025 as demand growth is staying healthy while construction wanes.

Overall, the outlook for industrial REITs is positive but significantly varying by product type and location. EGP is favorably positioned on both property specs and submarket concentration.

Asset Size

While a 7.1% vacancy is on the high end, that number varies greatly by property type. Assets below 100K square feet have substantially lower vacancy rates.

There are two reasons for this discrepancy:

  1. Most of the new construction had a large footprint
  2. Smaller assets are suitable for a broader range of tenants

The chart below marks shallow bay construction in green.

EGP’s focus on smaller properties has allowed it to evade the vacancy uptick. As of the most recent quarter, occupancy for EGP is 97.3%. They are also ideally positioned geographically.

Asset Location

The Marcus and Millichap mid-year industrial report is both informative and free, so I would highly recommend a read for anyone investing in industrial REITs. They ranked the top markets below.

These have an incredible overlap with EGP’s highest exposure markets. These markets were also confirmed to be strong by the Prologis (PLD) Q2'25 earnings call:

Chris Caton: SVP and Head of Research at Prologis

We are still going through this transition period. This sort of post-pandemic normalization, where the interior markets outperformed last year, remains a trend this year.

Interior markets are benefiting from the onshoring of manufacturing as well as evading port city weakness brought about by tariff trade uncertainty.

Caton continued:

Not all coastal markets are created equal. SoCal is weak, but Southern Florida, and Washington, D.C. are examples of greater resilience. Dallas comes to mind. The leading submarkets there are really firming. So you're starting to see a transition across a range of markets.

Hamid R. Moghadam, CEO, extended the list of strong markets:

I think Houston and Nashville are pretty strong.

It so happens that the markets Moghadam and Caton called out for strength are EGP’s four largest markets and make up nearly 40% of EGP’s revenues.

EGP’s top markets

Valuation

Consensus NAV estimates show EGP at a net asset value of $179.49

It's currently trading at about 90% of NAV.

From an earnings multiple perspective, EGP’s discounted valuation is hidden by the fact that its nominal AFFO multiple is among the highest in the sector. It trades at 21.8X 2026 AFFO compared to the sector average of 16.5X.

However, on a leverage-neutral valuation, it's among the cheapest. We plot the AFFO multiple on the Y axis and debt to capital on the X axis to approximate a leverage-neutral valuation.

The cold storage REITs fall far below the line indicating undervaluation, but they have a different, and in my opinion, less promising set of fundamentals.

Among the traditional industrial REITs, EGP is the only one below the line.

Balance Sheet

Historically, 30% to 40% debt to capital has been considered the optimal range for REITs because leverage amplifies earnings power, and that level typically has not incurred too much extra risk.

EGP is well below that level at 13.97% debt to capital. They have received some criticism for operating at such low leverage because they could have significantly higher cashflows if they levered up a bit.

Marshall Loeb discussed the low leverage at REITweek and is not opposed to taking it up slightly. He has just been waiting for the right opportunity.

These past two years have not been a good time to lever up for two reasons:

  1. The cost of debt is relatively high right now
  2. Cap rates are relatively low right now

So, the spreads attained in leveraging up to buy properties would be rather small.

We believe the dry powder is on the cusp of finding a home.

Development Opportunity

PLD discussed the shifting landscape in their Q2 earnings call:

Tim Arndt (PLD’s CFO)

Our build-to-suit starts for the first half totaled $1.1 billion, which is the largest start to a year that we have ever had.

Hamid Moghadam

And then there's finally build-to-suit activity, which I'm going to go on a limb and say, this is the strongest it's been in my career. So people who can plan in the long term are also, I think, thinking the way I'm thinking about it, which is they're looking at a couple of years by the time these projects are fully operational. And they look at the factors driving the long-term health of their business, like e-commerce and things like that, and they're feeling good about it. So — the only part of our business that is slow is leasing of spec space.

The industrial landscape is shifting from spec to build-to-suit. It's healthier for the whole sector as build-to-suits are already leased and therefore don't compete on leasing with currently available properties. Build-to-suits also start cash flowing faster.

East Group has full development capabilities, having built 269 properties since 1996. They have another 4 million square feet in development with a projected investment spend of $573 million.

With their balance sheet, they can build a whole lot more than that. One of the aspects I really like about conservative management is that it will do so if and only if it is significantly accretive.

Organic Growth

Consequent to being in higher-growth industrial markets and having the low-supply property type, leasing spreads are highly favorable. As EGP’s leases roll, they're renewing at much higher rates, resulting in consistently strong same-store NOI growth.

We anticipate similar growth to continue.

Consensus estimates call for AFFO/share growing to $10.52 by 2029.

Between developments and leasing spreads discussed above, this rapid growth seems entirely plausible.

Risks to EGP

Due to the balance sheet and generally conservative operations, EGP is arguably lower risk than peers, but it does leave the door open to getting outpaced. Specifically, EGP would benefit relatively less than peers from the following events:

  • Substantial decline in interest rates
  • Surge in Amazon-style e-commerce demand
  • Cap rate declines

Overall Take

EGP is positioned to grow AFFO per share at a roughly 10% annual pace. Combined with a 3.45% dividend yield, it offers mid-teens return potential if the AFFO multiple stays flat.

Capital gains can be realized faster if EGP returns toward a more normalized multiple appropriate for its growth and appropriate for its balance sheet.

This is a great company, and we are happy to scoop it up at such a discounted valuation. I have been waiting years for this kind of pricing on EGP.

data.title: Erosion Goes On For SPUU
data.description:
Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 2X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SPUU) may be a profitable swing trading instrument, but the 2X leverage factor is a source of drift. This article explains what "drift" means and reports it for 22 leveraged ETFs, with a focus on SPUU.

Why Do Leveraged ETFs Drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, and management costs. Beta-slippage is the main reason in equity-leveraged ETFs. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. On the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1

In the same time, the perfect leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%…

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21

…while the perfect 2X leveraged ETFs is up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44

Beta-slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one third of its value, instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667

Without a demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the larger the decay.

Moreover, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the decay. If we come back to the first example, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged. And the “perfect” -3X ETF?

(1 - 0.75) x (1 + 0.6) = 0.4

It is down 60%, suggesting by this example that leveraged bear ETFs are the riskiest in volatile markets.

Monthly And Yearly Drift Watchlist

There is no standard or universal definition of leveraged ETF drift. Mine is based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index on a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. Most of the time, this factor defines a daily objective relative to an underlying index. However, some dividend-oriented leveraged products have been defined with a monthly or quarterly objective.

Let’s start with some definitions. “Return” is the return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval, including dividends. “IndexReturn” is the return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval, including dividends. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval. It is calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (IndexReturn x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

“Decay” means negative drift. “Month” stands for 21 trading days, and “year” for 252 trading days.

Index

Ñ

Ticker

1-month Return

1-month Drift

1-year Return

1-year Drift

S&P 500

  • The leveraged bull biotechnology ETF (LABU) has the worst monthly decay of this list with a drift of -0.85%, while the leveraged bull semiconductors ETF (SOXL) has suffered the largest 12-month decay: -18.85%.
  • SDS, the -2x S&P 500 ETF, has the highest positive drift in one month (+0.57%), while the leveraged bear gold ETF (GLL) has the highest 12-month drift at +14.8%, in a large loss.
  • The drift of SPUU is negative on both periods.

Positive drift results from a steady trend in the underlying asset, whatever the trend direction. Negative drift comes with daily return volatility (“whipsaw”). For example, bull and bear leveraged ETFs in semiconductors and biotechnology show monthly and yearly decays, because stock prices in these industries are very volatile.

SPUU Drift History

Since its inception on 5/28/2014, SPUU has gained 661% (19.9% annualized), while SPY is at +301% (13.2% annualized). The ratio of total returns is above the daily leveraging factor, which looks good. However, a simulation with synthetic prices from January 2000 is more concerning: SPUU is at +620% and SPY at +586%. Over 25 years, the leveraged ETF (simulated) has hardly outperformed the non-leveraged one and shows a maximum drawdown of -89%.

The next chart plots the 12-month drift from January 2000, using synthetic prices. The historical average is -2.00%, while it is -2.22% for the 3x bull ETF UPRO (second chart below).

The difference looks minimal, but the simulation of UPRO since January 2000 results in a total return inferior to SPY’s: 308% (5.7% annualized), with a maximum drawdown of 98%.

Takeaway

SPUU is a profitable trading instrument in a bull market. Nonetheless, it suffers from decay when the S&P 500 alternates positive and negative daily returns. The 12-month drift of SPUU has been negative since January 2022 (except a few days). Even if SPUU is safer compared to UPRO or SPXL, investors should refrain from holding it for the long term.

data.title: Monthly Macro Recap (August 1, 2025)
data.description:
Introduction

Normally, I do not share this with the public. This is the top half of my newsletter. I decided this was important enough to share.

Newsletter

July 31st (Previous Month) — Targets (near-term, then 2025 year-end)

AU: $3289 ($3306) — $3100 to $3200, then $3400 to $3500

AG: $36.64 ($36.01) — $31 to $33, then $45 to $60.

HUI: 424 (418) — 350 to 380, then 600 to 650.

DXY: 99.8 (96.4) — 99 to 101, then 95 to 96.

S&P: 6339 (6204) — 6300 to 6400, then 4500 to 4000.

10-Yr: 4.3.5% (4.23%) — 3.8% to 4%, then 4.5 to 4.8%.

Oil: $69 ($65) — $60 to $65, then $90 to $95.

I remain convinced that the economy is slowing into a recession. Some economists believe that the 2020 downturn was a recession and a business cycle reset. If that was a recession, then most people didn’t feel it. Moreover, the stock market did an almost immediate V-Shaped recovery (only two down months and not two quarters). In fact, 2020 was a strong year for the stock market (see monthly S&P 500 chart below). Those are not stock market numbers that you normally see during a recession.

(Dbl-click for better resolution.)

The closest we came to a recession was from January 2022 to October 2022. That probably was a recession, but government spending juiced GDP numbers. Then we had another hiccup in September 2023, followed by a quick V-shaped recovery. And the last one was in April of this year, with the current V-shaped recovery.

So, in actuality, we have not had a proper recession since June of 2009, when the last recession ended (the last financial crisis of 2008). This current 15+ year business cycle is long in the tooth and the longest in over 100 years. I expect it to end soon. The only reason it has lasted this long is because of rampant manipulation by the Fed and fiscal dominance by the U.S. Government (massive budget deficits).

I believe the reason for this ongoing Fed manipulation and U.S. Government fiscal dominance is because of the fact that the U.S. economy can no longer grow on its own. Moreover, the manipulation and fiscal dominance (massive debt expansion) have created too much fragility in the economy to allow a recession. Basically, the Fed and the U.S. Government are digging themselves into a deeper and deeper hole.

This economic trap was always my thesis when I began buying gold and silver mining stocks. It was always an inevitability that the U.S. Government would spend until the bond market broke. In other words, they would expand debt until the bond market stopped working.

As investors in gold and silver mining stocks, we don’t need the debt bubble to pop. All we need is for it to begin to pop, which I think it has. Once you get close to the end, it is inevitable that you get into a doom loop, where the only choices are bad choices. At that point, gold sniffs it out and begins trending. I think that has already begun.

President Trump’s tariffs, in my view, are a sign of desperation. These tariffs are not being used to bring back manufacturing. They are being used to raise money because the U.S. Government bond market is in jeopardy. If Trump wanted to bring back manufacturing, he would do it on a company-by-company and sector-by-sector basis. It would be easy to force individual corporations to build plants in the U.S. For instance, all he would have to do is tell the European and Japanese auto makers to build 1 or 2 plants in the U.S. or else face 25% tariffs. He could do the same with the phone makers, appliance makers, or any sector.

The fact that Trump is willing to damage our trading partners' economies shows that he is desperate. You don’t hurt your customers if you don’t have to, and U.S. corporations get at least 40% of their profits overseas. Tariffs are bad for business, and Trump knows it.

Tariffs are only the tip of the iceberg for clues of U.S. economic trouble. The balance sheets of the large banks are a mess, with huge paper losses from bonds that are underwater. Plus, their commercial real estate loans have huge impending losses. Plus, their customers are defaulting on credit cards, auto loans, business loans, and mortgages. This is happening when the stock market keeps making all-time highs.

How can you have a banking system that is unhealthy and a stock market that is at ATHs? Only one way, liquidity. You flood the financial system with money ($2T budget deficits) and pretend that everything is normal. However, in actuality, you get a Potemkin economy that is ripe for a financial crisis.

We learned from the Japanese (in the 1990s) that you could inject large amounts of liquidity to prevent recessions. That is what we started doing after 9/11 in 2001. Subsequently, our national debt has risen from $5.7T on 9/11/2001 to $38T today. Yes, that’s not a typo. There’s your liquidity.

Because high liquidity can mask economic problems, it’s hard to know when the party ends. However, what we can do is look for signs. When the Fed recently lowered rates, the long end of the bond curve actually went up, which surprised everyone. That is the first clue that we are close.

Another clue is that the labor market is starting to crack. College graduates are having trouble finding jobs, as are high-paying professionals who have lost their jobs. New job creation was 19,000 in May and 14,000 in June. The July number is 73,000, but is likely to be revised lower. Note that 200,000 jobs is a healthy jobs market (see chart below).

The recent cost-of-living increase since 2020 is clearly starting to bite. People are selling their gold and silver to pay their bills. Turnover rates for apartments are on the rise. Housing sales for both new and existing are at recession levels. Auto sales are being impacted by tariffs.

Retail has struggled since 2020. Kohl's (KSS) is perhaps the canary that rolled over and died. Sales were at $20B prior to COVID in 2020. Today, sales are at $16B and languishing. Sales never came back. It’s been like that for many retail outlets.

The University of Michigan consumer confidence is at recession levels. For July, it was at 61.7. The average for 2017 was 96.8, and in 2018 it was 98.4. The PMI for manufacturing in July was 49.8. That’s a recessionary number.

The economy is hanging on, but it is clearly slowing. Wall Street is under the assumption that all the economy needs is lower interest rates, and all will be well. That’s a fantasy they are selling us. However, Powell will not lower rates dramatically between now and May, when his term is over. Even if he lowers them by 150 bps, that is not enough to fix all of the existing problems. Trump has suggested that we need rates down at least 300 bps to get interest rate relief.

I’ve never believed in the soft landing or no landing thesis. I’ve always expected a recession to arrive. While I can’t point to one particular data point that implies a recession is coming, I do expect some type of black swan, such as a Bear Sterns bankruptcy, to appear out of nowhere. I’m waiting for that next crisis, and I think it is near.

We are now entering weak seasonality. Mid-August to mid-October is always dicey for the stock market. Both 2022 and 2023 were volatile, with significant stock market corrections. Last year was not. Wall Street collectively holds its breath this time of year until we get to mid-October, when seasonality generally improves until year-end.

I do not think we will get a smooth run from now until mid-October like we did in 2024. The stock market has been on a tear since mid-April and is overdue for some type of correction. Perhaps it will only correct 5% to 8% and then we get another V-shaped recovery. Stay tuned; it could get dicey.

I still believe that the tariffs are the pin that is going to pop this "everything" bubble. Trump is playing with fire. The global economy is highly interrelated, especially in global finance. He is damaging a lot of weak economies with his tariffs. Europe has been in a recession since 2020, and these tariffs will hurt it. Same with Japan. When you hurt an economy, you damage its liquidity, whereby weaker companies cannot get credit.

The tariffs also damage the U.S. consumer, which makes up 70% of the US economy. The discretionary spending levels will get squeezed from these tariffs. The consumer will have less money to spend. Wall Street keeps saying, don’t worry, the tariffs won’t hurt corporate earnings. I think they will be wrong.

The wild card that I expect to appear is a trade war. So far, the only country that has pushed back was China, which raised its own reciprocal tariffs and cut-off rare earth exports. Trump immediately negotiated a truce with China on reciprocal tariffs, which is still in effect. But a deal with China has not been made.

Canada and Mexico have also not made trade deals yet. Will one of these countries fight back? Stay tuned. Just today, Trump placed a 25% tariff on India, plus a penalty if they continue to purchase Russian oil. He also mentioned that India is part of the BRICS, which is trying to destroy the dollar (his words). I don’t think Trump is done using tariffs to penalize BRICS countries.

I think we need to be aware of why Trump is using these tariffs. This goes all the way back to 1971, when President Nixon abandoned the gold standard. From 1971 until today, the U.S. has used a global trade policy that focused on free trade, which is essentially globalism. The U.S. gave up its manufacturing base and allowed our corporations to move their manufacturing plants offshore. Today, Apple makes all of its phones in foreign countries for this reason. In fact, today, zero phones, TVs, or appliances are made in the USA.

This pivot to globalism has had deleterious effects on the U.S. economy. The top 1% now own over 50% of the wealth. Wall Street now generates 20% of GDP. The middle class has watched its real income remain flat for 50 years. This is the first middle-class generation that is poorer than their parents. No longer can the middle class afford a new home. Home affordability has become a crisis in America.

But it gets worse. The U.S. government is trapped in a debt doom loop, whereby it has to borrow $100B to $500B every month. In Q3, 2025, it will borrow $1T. If it stops spending, the economy will crash. Conversely, if it keeps spending, the economy will also crash. A classic catch-22. We have reached a Humpty-Dumpty moment. Once the next recession appears, Humpty-Dumpty is going to fall. There will be no putting it back together again.

The fragility of the U.S. Government bond market has already started. Foreigners have stopped showing up at the monthly auctions on a net basis. Foreigners held 43% of U.S. Government bond debt 20 years ago. Today, it is down to 31% and trending lower. This means that U.S. investors (and the Fed) have become the main buyers. With such a huge bond supply, finding enough buyers is starting to become a problem.

Congress recently passed the Genius Act, which is another act of desperation. This crypto law allows stablecoin companies to purchase U.S. Treasury bonds at these auctions. This law essentially gives crypto stablecoins the blessing of the U.S. Government. Basically, we created another customer for our debt to remove pressure from the bond market.

Are you paying attention? That is two acts of desperation to remove pressure from the failing U.S. Government bond market.

As Robert Triffin so accurately predicted in the 1960s, the U.S. economy is doomed by having the global reserve currency. Triffin said that a reserve currency would drive manufacturing overseas and hollow out the middle class. Bingo. He was right. That tragedy is now playing out.

Next, enter Ludwig Von Mises, who wrote in the 1950s that a government cannot have a free lunch. Meaning that if a government borrows money, it either has to pay it back or else a debt bubble will materialize and eventually pop. Guess what? The U.S. Government has not only not paid back its debt, but it keeps borrowing more and more. This is the ultimate Von Mises debt bubble that has to pop soon.

The Ukraine War was the trigger for the end of the U.S. Era and the end of U.S. global hegemony. This actually goes back to 1963, with the Kennedy assassination. After that, the Deep State has fought one war after another, beginning in Vietnam in 1964. The most recent war against Russia in Ukraine (mostly funded by U.S. taxpayer dollars) is likely the final war. Why? Because now we are broke.

These wars were never about improving humanity, human rights, or making the world a better place. These wars all had the same objective: to maintain global hegemony and make some profit (for the 1% crowd). As my friend Raley said, foreign policy is always about money. It’s never about making the world a better place.

This final battle was always inevitable. Brzezinski’s epic book, The Grand Chessboard, clearly explains the U.S. dilemma. The U.S. either could allow Russia, China, and Europe to consolidate into a global economic bloc (Eurasia) or buy time and try to prevent that outcome (by making Russia the villain). Ultimately, the U.S. was going to lose its hegemony due to geography and demographics. That time has come.

If you study economic history, then you will know that empires end and global reserve currencies end. You will also know the signs when those outcomes occur. The biggest sign is debt, and the second is economic decline. The third is perhaps the most important, which is foreign policy failures.

Not only are all three apparent today for the U.S., but the third is highly apparent. With Trump’s tariff tirade, he is putting the U.S. into the crosshairs of a global rebellion. The rest of the world is not happy and is ready to hurt the U.S. If you are paying attention, you realize this will not end well. We have ostracized all of our allies. We have set ourselves up to fail.

Once the U.S. loses its position of dominant global hegemony, its debt bubble will pop, and lots of bad things will happen to the U.S. economy. Thus, Trump’s acts of desperation. Capiche?

Gold is the winner in this outcome because the U.S. dollar and U.S. bonds are both going to contract in value, and investors will need a place to hide. Amazingly, Wall Street has not figured this out yet. Once they do, there will be a run to gold, silver, and the miners. I’ve known this for 35 years. Now you do, too. Welcome to the party.

I don’t want this outcome, but it is inevitable. Read my book, The Path Forward, for an idea of how this unfolds. Ironically, this massive economic shift is not the big event. When the economic shift happens (and it will), it won’t be the biggest shift we experience. What’s coming will be shocking and not what everyone expects. Hold on tight; the ride is going to get bumpy.

I will say this: in my opinion, America is over. This will be apparent in the next few years, especially when we don’t recover from this coming recession. What does that mean—America is over? Exactly that. It’s over. The American experiment with our current Constitution is done. We will likely now breakup into regions, create new countries, and start over. It won’t be easy.

I want to make one final point. I don’t base my analysis or expectation on what other analysts say or write. Furthermore, I do respect and pay attention to people like Luke Groman, Jeremy Grantham, Ed Dowd, Stanley Druckenmiller, Jeffrey Gundlach, Eric Sprott, Francis Hunt, Simon Hunt, and Lyn Alden. While most analysts don’t understand what is happening today, those nine people surely do. Many of these analysts are somewhat in alignment with what I have written above, but that is only a coincidence. I’ve come to my analysis on my own. I follow my own instincts, and so should you. Don’t follow other people. Follow yourself.

* * * * *

I recently posted two articles on Seeking Alpha (they should be free to the public), and I posted both as PDFs on the GSD forum. They are easy to find on the forum. Just click on the Member List, find me, then click on Posts. The two articles are the Top 10 Silver Producers and the Top 10 Silver Developers.

Finally, on the GSD forum, under the thread Good Resources is a locked post at the top called Checklists. These include 3 checklists for picking Producers, Developers, and Explorers. They are highly useful and were created from years of experience.

data.title: Cboe Global Markets, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cboe Global Markets, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Natural Gas Steadies Near $3 As U.S. Futures Weaken Despite Europe's Supply Risks
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​Natural gas futures are trading at $3.07, down 0.93%, extending a bearish trend that began in late June. Price action remains confined within a contracting triangle, with a breakdown now threatening the ascending trendline that has held since May 2024.

Repeated failures to reclaim the 20-day and 50-day EMAs, at $3.25 and $3.39 respectively, reinforce near-term weakness. The 100-day EMA rejection at $3.35 further underscored fading momentum. A decisive drop below $3 could pave the way for a slide toward $2.75, with subsequent support around $2.50 and $2.30. The long-term floor near $1.88 could re-enter the picture if selling accelerates for NG.

To reverse the trend, bulls must reclaim control above $3.25 and challenge the descending upper boundary near $3.45. RSI remains near 40.52, suggesting momentum is still bearish but not yet at extremes.

Europe’s energy risks mount amid LNG delays and sanctions rhetoric

On the global stage, European gas sentiment is diverging from U.S. softness due to tightening fundamentals. Output disruptions at Norway’s Troll gas field and a delayed Hammerfest LNG restart have limited near-term supply, lifting European futures back above €35/MWh after touching a 12-week low. The geopolitical landscape also adds pressure. President Trump’s ultimatum to Russia, which is a ceasefire deadline in Ukraine paired with threats of secondary sanctions on energy buyers, has increased fears of LNG trade disruption. Markets are assessing whether countries like China and India could be forced to scale back Russian imports, reshaping flows at a time when Europe remains vulnerable.

Egypt’s plans to boost LNG imports through 2030 add a long-term demand driver, while near-term warmer weather in Northwest Europe may support cooling-related consumption. However, for U.S. contracts, the technical picture remains dominant in the absence of strong domestic catalysts.

In our previous update, we highlighted natural gas's struggle to regain strength above $3.35 despite supportive global cues. The latest triangle breakdown confirms growing bearish pressure, though European demand and weather patterns may offer stabilizing factors in the near term. Structural support now depends on holding the $3 floor.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Boot Barn: Valuation Doesn't Support Guided H2 Deceleration
data.description:
Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BOOT) reported the company's fiscal Q1 results on the 31st of July. The western footwear and apparel retailer showed incredible sales momentum, also trickling into rapid earnings growth. Yet, even the raised FY 2026 guidance raises questions about Boot Barn's future performance in the more challenging tariff landscape.

I remained at a Sell rating in my previous January article, titled "Boot Barn: Valuation Remains Too High Despite Outstanding SSS." Since then, Boot Barn's stock has returned 5%, nearly on par with S&P 500's 4% return.

Boot Barn's Momentum Continued in Q1

As could be expected, Boot Barn reported solid fiscal Q1 results from the April-June period. Revenues reached $504.1 million, even showing accelerated 19.1% growth. The topline result also beat Wall Street's consensus by $8.9 million, as Boot Barn's critical same-store sales metric showed another fantastic quarter. This time, same-store sales grew by 9.4%, even above previous quarters' already rapid growth in the metric, as consumers have remained highly interested in Boot Barn. The same-store sales growth was driven by 8.5% growth in transactions and a 1% gain in units per transaction, while pricing remained flat.

Strong demand showed up in Boot Barn's profitability as well—strong demand drove a 210 basis point improvement in the gross margin to 39.1%. On top of strong pricing power that drove merchandise margin gains, Boot Barn's rapid growth has brought efficiencies from scale in buying, occupancy, and distribution. Operating expenses scaled in line with revenues, resulting in operating income of $70.7 million at a 14.0% margin, up by 40.8% year-on-year. The $1.74 EPS beat Wall Street's consensus by $0.20.

Altogether, Q1 financials were still very strong. Boot Barn's reported earnings haven't shown any signs of slowing momentum but rather even showing acceleration in same-store sales growth. Such strong store performance sets up a good platform for Boot Barn to expand on as the company still targets to double its store footprint. Boot Barn has continued to open up new stores, including 14 openings in Q1 alone to a total of 473.

The Raised Guidance Still Suggests Momentum to Halt

Boot Barn also raised the FY 2026 guidance with the Q1 report due to a strong start to the fiscal year. The company now expects revenues of $2100–2180 million, with the upper bound up by $30 million. At the midpoint, revenues are guided to grow by 12.0%. Operating income is expected at $239-277 million, with the upper bound up by $11 million from the previous guidance.

Despite a raised guidance, Boot Barn's FY 2026 expectations suggest rapid deceleration in same-store sales growth—Q2 is still expected to show good 6.5% same-store sales growth, but the metric is expected to flatline afterwards in H2 even at the guidance range's high end. The sales guidance's upper bound still only assumes 3.5% same-store sales growth in FY 2026. When considering Boot Barn's strong 11.7% same-store sales growth in July, still showing impressive momentum, the sudden slowdown expectation seems intriguing. Halted momentum in the metric would make Boot Barn's overall growth much less attractive.

Boot Barn attributes the expected H2 weakness to macroeconomic uncertainty around tariffs—the consumer reaction to Boot Barn's pricing increases is uncertain with demand elasticity yet to be put to the test. The company ultimately expects higher prices to result in softer consumer demand, a sentiment that is echoed by most company executives. With an already uncertain consumer, demand could see notable pressure ahead as prices are increased to protect margins. Yet, Boot Barn at the same time expects the merchandise margin to decline slightly in H2 as the company isn't fully shifting tariffs into pricing.

The guidance could still ultimately turn out to be conservative—Boot Barn continued to see good momentum in July despite gradual price increase execution, and such a sharp trend halt seems like a very conservative expectation. At the same time, the guidance does raise concerns about Boot Barn's future growth in the more challenging tariff-weighed macroeconomic backdrop. The company already has very healthy traffic, making further growth in same-store sales increasingly hard to achieve with already challenging comps.

Boot Barn's Valuation Is Too Expensive Again

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Boot Barn. I assume FY 2026 financials to come in slightly above the raised guidance with 15.1% revenue growth. Afterwards, I again expect a similar gradual growth slowdown with a total FY 2025-FY 2035 CAGR of 8.5%.

Boot Barn's strong margin gains should continue as price increases mitigate tariffs and the company continues to grow. I again expect the EBIT margin to ultimately reach 14%, and that Boot Barn slightly beats the FY 2026 guidance.

I again assume quite a good cash flow conversion, enabled by great new store economics.

The estimates put Boot Barn's fair value estimate at $124.7, 28% below the stock price at the time of writing. After the stock has regained previous losses, I now believe Boot Barn to be overvalued again. The company would need to show very rapid and capital-efficient growth with a long runway for the stock to provide adequate long-term returns.

The fair value estimate is up from $118.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.95%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don't expect Boot Barn to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent July estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.39. With a liquidity premium of 3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.95%:

Takeaway

Boot Barn's Q2 report was outstanding through solid sales momentum, but the FY 2026 guidance still suggests a very sharp performance slowdown in H2. Tariffs are expected to cause weaker consumer demand, pressuring same-store sales growth momentum and merchandise margins. As I estimate the stock to be overvalued, I remain with a Sell rating for Boot Barn.

data.title: Jobs Day In Four Charts
data.description:
While payrolls announced by the BLS for July missed (+73k versus +104k estimated), the bigger story was revisions. A combined 258k jobs were revised out of May and June numbers, taking those months’ NFP prints to +19k and +14k respectively. Almost half of the revisions lower came from state and local government education, which accounts for less than 7% of total employment.

While jobs growth has slowed, there’s been no major uptick in unemployment due to job loss or more workers only part-time because they can’t find full-time work.

Labor income (employment times wages times hours) was also up over 7% annualized on the month and is up at 3.7% annualized on a 3m/3m basis that is similar to 2019’s nominal growth rate. Labor income has certainly slowed, but it’s not cratering in a way that would drive sharp pullbacks in spending despite the slow jobs growth.

Since the Global Financial Crisis, labor market data has broadly been viewed as an insight to the demand side of the labor market. But the current context is quite different: unemployment remains low, while labor supply is falling. The last 3 months saw a 3.3mm annualized decline in the labor force, driven by immigration policy and to a lesser extent demographics. As a result, slow job creation is much less concerning than when labor supply is rising.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
